chapter_context.summary: Records Jonah's poetic prayer of thanksgiving from the belly of the fish, recalling his descent into the depths, weeds wrapped about his head, and his transition from despair to praise of God's salvation, culminating in the fish vomiting him onto dry land.

outline.text: Cry from the Belly of Sheol: Desolate Waves and Billows Passing Over Jonah
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Cry from the Belly of Sheol: Desolate Waves and Billows Passing Over Jonah (2:1-4), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Jonah.

outline.text: Descent to the Earth's Bars: Weeds Wrapped Around His Head and the Remembrance of the Lord
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Descent to the Earth's Bars: Weeds Wrapped Around His Head and the Remembrance of the Lord (2:5-7), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Jonah.

outline.text: Contrast with Vain Idolatry: Poetic Vow of Thanksgiving and the Declaration 'Salvation is of the LORD'
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Contrast with Vain Idolatry: Poetic Vow of Thanksgiving and the Declaration 'Salvation is of the LORD' (2:8-9), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Jonah.

outline.text: The Great Fish Vomiting Jonah Out Upon the Dry Land at God's Command
outline.explanation: This section focuses on The Great Fish Vomiting Jonah Out Upon the Dry Land at God's Command (2:10), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Jonah.

key_themes.title: The Universality of Divine Mercy
key_themes.description: Jonah shows that God's grace and compassion are not limited to one covenant nation (Israel) but extend to all humanity, including foreign enemies like Assyria.

key_themes.title: The Futility of Fleeing God
key_themes.description: The narrative makes clear that God's sovereignty is absolute over creation, storm, fish, plant, and worm, rendering human attempts to escape His call completely futile.

devotional.question: Reflecting on the events and outcomes in Chapter 2, what does Jonah's reaction to God's mercy tell us about our own tendency to hoard grace and judge others?
How can you cultivate a heart that is aligned with God's compassion for the 'great cities' and lost people in our modern world?

cultural_historical.title: Nineveh and the Assyrian Empire
cultural_historical.text: Nineveh was the magnificent and brutal capital of the Assyrian Empire. To Israel, Assyria was a cruel, terrifying, and hated oppressor, which explains Jonah's extreme reluctance to preach a message that might lead to their salvation.
